key_insight: Radially varying band-gap collapse around a swift heavy ion track in Al2O3 creates a transient metal-like core and changes how energy is deposited into the lattice.
engineering_relevance: Track-damage predictions for ion-irradiated oxides should account for spatially heterogeneous electronic structure, not just uniform electron-to-atom energy transfer.
method: TREKIS Monte Carlo simulates initial electronic and ionic excitation after ion passage.
DFT-based molecular dynamics tracks band-structure evolution in the ion track.
Radial energy-to-lattice heating is estimated using the local band-gap reduction profile.
evidence_signal: For a 700 MeV Bi ion, the abstract reports band-gap collapse within ~2 nm, reduced gaps at ~3–5 nm, and little change beyond ~5 nm, leading to more discontinuous damage patterns than uniform-deposition models.
watch_item: The approach depends on coupled simulation assumptions and material-specific band-gap dynamics, so transferability to other ions, energies, or oxides needs validation.
paper_title: Effect of radially heterogeneous band gap collapse on formation of swift heavy ion tracks in Al2O3
